elements.sent-2411-001: We employ a semi-analytical methodology to estimate the dark matter halo spin of HI gas-rich galaxies in the Arecibo Legacy Fast Alfa Survey and investigate the relationship between halo spin and the proximity of galaxies to large-scale filaments.
elements.sent-2411-002: We exclude galaxies with low HI signal-to-noise ratios, those potentially influenced by velocity dispersions, and those affiliated with galaxy clusters/groups.
elements.sent-2411-003: Additionally, we apply a mass-weighting technique to ensure consistent mass distribution across galaxy samples at varying distances from filaments.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our analysis reveals, for the first time, a subtle yet statistically significant correlation between halo spin and filament distance in observational data, indicating higher spins closer to filaments.
elements.sent-2411-005: This suggests that the tidal forces exerted by filaments may impact the spin of dark matter halos.
elements.sent-2411-006: The spin of dark matter halos plays a crucial role in shaping the evolution and characteristics of galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-007: Hydrodynamical simulations (e.g., \citealt{Kim13,Jiang192 ) and semi-analytic galaxy formation models (e.g., \citealt{Mo98 ) suggest that halo spin significantly influences the size and density of baryonic matter distribution, particularly in massive late-type galaxies.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: While the impact of halo spin on low-mass galaxies remains a topic of debate \citep[e.g.,][]{Yang23 , studies on specific dwarf galaxies, such as ultra-diffuse galaxies (\citealt{Rong17a,Amorisco16,Liao19,Benavides23 ), indicate that halo spin may have a notable effect on the distribution of baryonic matter in dwarf galaxies.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Therefore, understanding the influence of halo spin on baryonic matter within galaxies and their co-evolution is crucial for advancing our understanding of galaxy formation models.
elements.sent-2411-010: Moreover, in addition to its connection with internal galaxy properties, halo spin is also linked to the large-scale structure of the universe.
elements.sent-2411-011: Halo spin is thought to originate from tidal torques induced by large-scale structures, resulting from gravitational interactions with neighboring structures (e.g., \citealt{Peebles69,White84 ) or through mergers (e.g., \citealt{Gardner01,Vitvitska02,Hetznecker06,Maller02 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Simulation studies suggest that halos exhibit faster spins in stronger tidal fields, with a more pronounced effect observed in more massive halos (\citealt{Wang11 ).}
elements.sent-2411-013: As halos transition from the linear to non-linear phases towards virialization, the impact of tidal torque diminishes.
elements.sent-2411-014: During this transition, the flow field surrounding halos displays non-zero vorticity, which is critical in determining halo angular momentum and leading to alignment between halo spin and vorticity (e.g., \citealt{Liberskind13 ).}
elements.sent-2411-015: Theoretically, a correlation between halo spin and large-scale filaments should exist due to the strong link between vorticity and filament direction.
elements.sent-2411-016: However, observational evidence suggests that the correlation between halo spin and filaments, particularly in late-type galaxies, is minimal and, in some instances, almost non-existent.
elements.sent-2411-017: The current understanding of the relationship between halo spin and the environment remains uncertain, with conflicting results from observations and simulations.
elements.sent-2411-018: Understanding dark matter halo spin and its relationship with large-scale structures is essential for unraveling the formation and evolution of galaxies in the universe.
elements.sent-2411-019: In this study, we utilize a semi-analytic approach to estimate halo spin for a substantial sample of HI-bearing galaxies from an HI survey and investigate the dependence of halo spin on galaxy distances to large-scale filaments.
elements.sent-2411-020: Section~\ref{sec:2 presents the dataset and describes the methodology for estimating halo spin.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Section~\ref{sec:3 provides a statistical analysis of galaxy halo spins across different filament distance ranges.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our findings are summarized in section~\ref{sec:4 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: The selection of galaxies for this study is based on the extensive Arecibo Legacy Fast Alfa Survey \citep[ALFALFA;][]{Giovanelli05,Haynes18 , a wide-ranging extragalactic HI survey covering approximately 6,600 deg$^2$ at high Galactic latitudes.}
elements.sent-2411-024: A comprehensive catalog \citep[$\alpha.$100;][]{Haynes18 released by the ALFALFA collaboration includes about 31,500 sources with radial velocities below 18,000 km s$^{-1}$, providing essential properties such as signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the HI spectrum, cosmological distance, 50\% peak width of the HI line ($W_{50}$) corrected for instrumental broadening, and the HI mass ($M_{\rm{HI }$), among other parameters.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Further details on these properties and their uncertainties can be found in \cite{Haynes18 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: To augment the dataset, ALFALFA galaxies have been cross-referenced with SDSS data \citep{Alam15 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Previous investigations by \cite{Durbala20 have estimated stellar masses $M_{\star}$ for ALFALFA galaxies with optical counterparts using different methods, with a preference for the stellar mass derived from spectral energy distribution (SED) fitting.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In cases where UV or infrared data are lacking for SED fitting, the stellar mass based on $g-i$ color is utilized, with any discrepancies in stellar mass among these methods considered negligible.
elements.sent-2411-029: The rotation velocity is computed as $V_{\rm{rot =W_{50 /2/\sin\phi$, where $\phi$ denotes the inclination of the HI disk.}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: When resolved HI data is unavailable, the optical apparent axis ratio $b/a$ from \cite{Durbala20 is employed to estimate the HI disk inclination $\phi$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The calculation involves $\sin\phi=\sqrt{(1-(b/a)^2)/(1-q_0^2)}$ (if $b/a \leq q_0$, we set $\phi=90^{\circ}$), with a fixed intrinsic thickness $q_0\sim 0.2$ \citep{Tully09,Giovanelli97,Li21 for massive galaxies, and $q_0\sim 0.4$ (\citealt{Rong24 ) for low-mass galaxies with $M_{\star <10^{9.5}\ M_{\odot}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: To ensure accuracy, galaxies with inclinations $\phi<50^{\circ}$ and low SNR$<10$ are excluded from the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-033: Additionally, galaxies exhibiting `single-horned' HI line profiles, indicating dominance of velocity dispersion over regular rotation, are identified using the kurtosis parameter $k_4>-1.0$ following \cite{Hua24 and \cite{ElBadry18 .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Only galaxies with double-horned HI profiles are considered in this study, excluding dispersion-dominated systems.
elements.sent-2411-035: The final sample comprises approximately $3,280$ galaxies, with stellar masses ranging from $10^7$ to $10^{11}$ $\rm M_{\odot}$, as depicted in panel~a of Fig.~\ref{fig1 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Under the assumption of an isothermal sphere model for the galaxy's dark matter halo and neglecting baryonic matter effects, the halo spin ($\lambda_{\rm{h }$) can be expressed as (\citealt{Hernandez07 ): \begin{equation}\lambda_{\rm{h}}\simeq 21.8 \frac{R_{\rm{HI,d}}/{\rm kpc}}{(V_{\rm{rot}}/{\rm km s^{-1}})^{3/2}}, \label{sam_HI}\end{equation} Here, $V_{\rm{rot }$ represents the halo's rotation velocity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The scale length of the HI disk, $R_{\rm{HI,d }$, is determined assuming a thin gas disk in centrifugal balance \citep{Mo98 , characterized by an exponential surface density profile: \begin{equation}\Sigma_{\rm{HI}}(R)=\Sigma_{{\rm{HI}},0} {\rm{exp}}(-R/R_{{\rm{HI,d}}}),\end{equation} where $\Sigma_{{\rm{HI ,0}$ denotes the central surface density of the HI disk.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The total HI mass $M_{\rm{HI }$ is linked to the scale length by \begin{equation}M_{\rm{HI}} = 2 \pi \Sigma_{{\rm{HI}},0} R_{{\rm{HI,d}}}^2 \label{HIeq_mass}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Furthermore, we define the HI radius $r_{\rm{HI }$ as the radius at which the HI surface density reaches $1\ \rm M_{\odot}\rm{pc^{-2 }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The estimation of $r_{\rm{HI }$ is guided by the observed correlation between $r_{\rm{HI }$ and $M_{\rm{HI }$, as indicated by empirical studies: $\log r_{\rm{HI =0.51\log M_{\rm{HI -3.59$ \citep{Wang16,Gault21 .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Hence, at $r_{\rm{HI }$, we have \begin{equation}\Sigma_{{\rm{HI}},0} {\rm{exp}}(-r_{\rm{HI}}/R_{{\rm{HI,d}}})=1\ \rm M_{\odot}\rm{pc^{-2}}. \label{HIeq_3}\end{equation} Using equations~(\ref{HIeq_mass ) and (\ref{HIeq_3 ), we can determine the value of $R_{{\rm{HI,d }$ for each galaxy in our sample, thereby enabling the estimation of the halo spin.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: For each galaxy, we identify its corresponding large-scale filament using the filament catalog compiled by \cite{Tempel14a .}
elements.sent-2411-043: This catalog contains the distance of each SDSS galaxy to the nearest filament spine, denoted as $d_{\rm{gf }$, which we utilize to explore the relationship between halo spin and filament proximity.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The catalog also provides the richness $N$ of the galaxy group to which each galaxy belongs.
elements.sent-2411-045: In our analysis, we focus on galaxies with $N=1$, selecting isolated HI-bearing galaxies to investigate their halo spins.
elements.sent-2411-046: To further refine our analysis, we divide our sample of HI-bearing galaxies into subgroups based on their proximity to filaments: $d_{\rm{gf }\leq 1.0$~Mpc$/h$, $1.0<d_{\rm{gf }\leq 3.0$~Mpc$/h$, and $d_{\rm{gf > 3.0$~Mpc$/h$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Typically, a distance of $d_{\rm{gf }\leq 1$~Mpc$/h$ corresponds to the approximate `boundary' of a filament \citep{Wang24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The stellar mass distributions of the three subsamples are presented in panel~a of Fig.~\ref{fig1 , showing distinct differences supported by low $p$-values from Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) tests.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Given the known relationship between halo spin and galaxy mass, it is crucial to ensure that the stellar mass distributions across the three subsamples are comparable to investigate the impact of environment on halo spins.
elements.sent-2411-050: To achieve this, we employ a mass-weighting control method.
elements.sent-2411-051: Initially, we establish the stellar mass distribution of the $d_{\rm{gf }\leq 1.0$~Mpc$/h$ subsample as the reference.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Subsequently, we assign weights to the other subsamples using the equation: \begin{equation}w_{x}(m_{\star})=f_{d_{\rm{gf}}\leq 1.0}(m_{\star})/f_{x}(m_{\star}),\end{equation} where $w_{x (m_{\star )$ and $f_{x (m_{\star )$ represent the weights and fraction of galaxies in subsample $x$ with stellar masses between $m_{\star}$ and $m_{\star +dm_{\star}$, respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: We then randomly select $n(m_{\star )$ galaxies from the subsample $x$ within the stellar mass range $m_{\star}$ to $m_{\star +dm_{\star}$ using \begin{equation}n(m_{\star})=w_{x}(m_{\star})/{\rm max}(w_{x}(m_{\star}))*n_x(m_{\star}),\end{equation} where $n_x(m_{\star )$ denotes the number of galaxies in subsample $x$ within the stellar mass interval $m_{\star}$ to $m_{\star +dm_{\star}$, and ${\rm max (w_{x (m_{\star ))$ is the maximum value of $w_{x (m_{\star )$ across different mass bins.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The stellar mass distributions of the three subsamples post mass-weighting are compared in panel~b of Fig.~\ref{fig1 .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Subsequently, we utilize the mass-weighted subsamples to examine the relationship between halo spin and $d_{\rm{gf }$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: In Fig.~\ref{compare , we present a comparative analysis of halo spin distributions across three distinct subsamples.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Our results reveal a subtle yet statistically significant correlation between the spin properties of galaxy halos and their proximity to large-scale filaments.
elements.sent-2411-058: Specifically, the median spins $\log\lambda_{\rm{h }$ are determined as $-0.825\pm 0.000, -0.858\pm 0.001, -0.861\pm 0.000$ for the subsamples characterized by $d_{\rm{gf }\leq 1.0$~Mpc$/h$, $1.0<d_{\rm{gf }\leq 3.0$~Mpc$/h$, and $d_{\rm{gf > 3.0$~Mpc$/h$, respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The uncertainties in the median values are derived as $\sigma_{{\lambda /\sqrt{N}$, where $N$ represents the sample size and $\sigma_{{\lambda }$ signifies the standard deviation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Notably, the disparity in median $\log\lambda_{\rm{h }$ between the subsamples of $d_{\rm{gf }\leq 1.0$~Mpc$/h$ and $1.0<d_{\rm{gf < 3.0$~Mpc$/h$ is statistically significant at a confidence level of approximately $30\sigma$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The two-sample K-S tests also yield small $p$-values further corroborating the observed distinctions in halo spin distributions among the three subsamples, as depicted in Fig.~\ref{compare .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Our findings suggest a discernible association between halo spin and the proximity to filaments, particularly highlighting the substantial divergence in halo spins between galaxies situated within filaments with $d_{\rm{gf }\leq 1.0$~Mpc$/h$ and those positioned outside filaments.}
elements.sent-2411-063: This observation underscores the potential influence of large-scale filament tidal fields on galaxy halo spin.
elements.sent-2411-064: Notably, the trend of increasing halo spins in denser environments aligns with previous studies based on $N$-body simulations \citep{Wang11,Hahn07b .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth, angle=0]{f1.pdf} \caption{The distributions of stellar masses of the galaxy subsamples with $d_{\rm{gf}}\leq 1.0$~Mpc$/h$ (red), $1.0<d_{\rm{gf}}\leq 3.0$~Mpc$/h$ (cyan), and $d_{\rm{gf}}> 3.0$~Mpc$/h$ (blue). Panels~a and b correspond to the original galaxy samples and samples after mass-weighting method, respectively. } \label{fig1}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth, angle=0]{f2.pdf} \caption{The comparison between the halo spin distributions of subsamples with $d_{\rm{gf}}\leq 1.0$~Mpc$/h$ (red), $1.0<d_{\rm{gf}}\leq 3.0$~Mpc$/h$ (cyan), and $d_{\rm{gf}}> 3.0$~Mpc$/h$ (blue). The small $p$-values from K-S tests of comparing the halo spin distributions indicate significant spin distribution differences.} \label{compare}
elements.sent-2411-065: We have employed a semi-analytical method to assess the halo spins of the HI-bearing galaxy sample obtained from ALFALFA and explored the potential correlation between spin values and the proximity of galaxies to large-scale filaments.
elements.sent-2411-066: To enhance the precision of our investigation, we have excluded galaxies within galaxy clusters/groups and those potentially affected by velocity dispersions.
elements.sent-2411-067: Furthermore, we have considered the influence of galaxy masses on the variations in halo spin.
elements.sent-2411-068: Our results indicate a subtle yet statistically significant relationship between halo spins and the distances of galaxies to filaments, implying a potential influence of large-scale filament tidal fields on galaxy halo spin.
elements.sent-2411-069: It is important to note that optical inclinations were employed in this study to estimate the rotation velocities and halo spins of galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-070: This method introduces uncertainties due to small misalignments ${\rm d}\phi$ between optical and HI inclinations observed in various galaxies \citep{Hunter12,Oh15 and simulations \citep{Nelson18,Nelson19,Vogelsberger14 .}}
elements.sent-2411-071: While these uncertainties may slightly attenuate any potential halo spin dependence on filament distance, the majority ($\gtrsim 70\%$) of galaxies exhibit ${\rm d}\phi<20^{\circ}$, indicating a moderate impact of misalignment.
